message.content: Adversaries may achieve persistence by exploiting the Windows screensaver functionality, which triggers execution after a period of user inactivity. Screensavers are executable files with a .scr extension, located in specific system paths. By manipulating registry settings, attackers can configure the screensaver to run malicious code instead of the intended screensaver program. This technique allows malware to execute without user awareness, maintaining a foothold on the system. Common exploitation methods include modifying registry keys to point to malicious executables and ensuring these settings persist across reboots. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and restricting changes to screensaver settings in the registry, employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized executables from running, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect unusual screensaver activity. Regular audits of system configurations and user activity can also help identify and prevent this type of persistence mechanism.
